title: Contact printing on silk
subtitle: Bundling leaves and flowers against damp silk and steaming to transfer plant colour and texture
excerpt: Contact printing, also called eco-printing, places plant material directly against damp fabric, bundles it tightly, and steams or simmers it. Heat and pressure transfer plant pigments and tannins to the cloth surface, leaving ghost prints of the plant's shape and colour. Silk is the most receptive base cloth for contact printing due to its protein fibre structure and smooth surface. No mordanting is required for basic results, though an alum or iron pre-mordant deepens the prints.
sourceNotes: Contact printing and eco-printing on silk are documented in contemporary natural dyeing and surface design practice. Method is observation-led and consistent with current workshop instruction in the field.

content.text: , the plant does the work: its own pigments and tannins transfer under heat and pressure. The
technique keeps plant and cloth in contact during steaming, which is what determines how sharp the final prints are.

items.name: Scoured silk fabric (habotai or crepe de chine)
Fresh or pressed leaves and flowers (eucalyptus, rose, fern, oak)
Alum mordant solution (optional, for stronger prints)
Wooden or metal rod for bundling
String or rubber bands to bind the bundle
Large pot for steaming with a lid

content.text: For basic prints, simply scour the silk in warm water with a small amount of washing-up liquid to remove any sizing. Rinse thoroughly and keep damp. For stronger, more colour-fast prints, soak the damp silk in a dilute alum solution (10-15 g per litre) for 30 minutes, then remove without rinsing. The alum improves the bonding of plant pigments to the silk surface.
Step 2: Lay out the plants and fold the silk

Lay the damp silk flat on a clean surface. Arrange leaves and flowers on one half of the fabric, vein-side down (the underside of a leaf transfers better than the smooth upper surface). Fold the other half of the silk over the top to sandwich the plants. For a mirror-print effect, fold so that both halves contact the same plant material; for a single-sided print, keep the plant on one layer only.

Roll the layered silk tightly around the rod, pressing firmly as you roll to keep the plant material in close contact with the fabric. Bind the roll with string, wrapping tightly at intervals of 3-4 cm. Place the bundle in a steaming pot above boiling water, cover with a lid, and steam for 60-90 minutes. Top up the water halfway through if needed.

Allow the bundle to cool completely before unrolling. Opening a hot bundle can smear the prints. Remove the plant material carefully, peel it away from the silk, and rinse the silk gently in cool water. Dry in the shade. The prints will lighten slightly as they dry.

items.fix: Roll and bind the bundle as tightly as possible. Choose high-tannin plants: eucalyptus, rose (leaves and petals), Japanese maple, and oak produce the strongest prints. Low-tannin plants such as grass and most herbs print faintly.
items.cause: Bundle not tight enough, or plants did not contain enough tannin to print well
items.problem: The prints are faint and ghost-like with no definition

items.fix: Let the bundle cool fully before opening. Press the plants firmly onto the silk before folding and rolling, and avoid allowing them to slide during the rolling step.
items.cause: Bundle opened while still hot, or plants shifted during rolling
items.problem: The prints smeared into an indistinct blur

items.fix: Iron contamination saddens all colours to grey. Use a stainless steel pot and fresh (not hard tap) water. If your tap water is iron-rich, use filtered or collected rainwater.
items.cause: Iron contamination from the pot or from iron-rich water reacting with plant tannins
items.problem: Only grey-brown marks appear with no defined plant shape
